'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == <!-- {{Commonscat|}} --> {{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}

revision.text:
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == <!-- {{Commonscat|}} --> {{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}

However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.

== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|函館山・立待岬から見た大森浜（[[2003年]]9月）]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=大森浜・啄木小公園付近|width=250}}
'''大森浜'''（おおもりはま）は、[[北海道]][[函館市]]の[[海岸]]である。
== 概要 == [[函館山]]を[[陸繋島]]とし、[[山麓]]より千代台の[[台地]]の縁（[[函館段丘]]）までの長さ約3,000m、幅約600m（[[埋立地]]を除く）の[[陸繋砂州]]<ref name="函館市史銭亀沢編_152-153">函館市史 銭亀沢編 pp.152-153</ref>の東側<ref>函館市史通説編第4巻 pp.839-843</ref>、[[住吉漁港]]より[[湯の川温泉 (北海道)|湯の川温泉]]、[[松倉川]][[河口]]付近までの[[津軽海峡]]に面していている[[海浜]]である<ref name="フィルムコミッション">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "大森浜"] はこだてフィルムコミッション 2024年3月2日閲覧</ref>。長さ約6,000m、最大幅約70mで干満差は約1.1メートル<ref name="スナガニ2017">五嶋聖治 "北限のスナガニの季節的な砂浜利用パターン：啄木はスナガニに出会えたか？" 日本ベントス学会誌 71: 83–89 2017年 pp.83-89</ref>。
かつて、この一帯（現在の高盛町、日の出町）には津軽海峡から運ばれてできた高さ30メートル、東西1キロメートル、南北300メートルもの<ref name=column48>[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm 『函館市史』通説編第4巻 コラム48「大森浜の砂山」] - 公立はこだて未来大学</ref>[[砂丘]]「[[大森山砂丘]]」があって<ref name="函館市史銭亀沢編_152-153"/>、大森は、その砂の盛り上がる「'''大盛り'''」より名付けられた<ref>函館市観光部編 [https://www.hakobura.jp/spots/607 "大森浜（啄木小公園）"] はこぶら 2025年3月7日閲覧</ref>。[[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）の今でも砂が風で巻き上げられ周辺の道路や駐車場に積もる現象がみられる<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "雨・風強まる函館市内からの報告 砂浜から巻き上げられた砂がたまって駐車場が…"] 北海道テレビ放送 2023年11月17日更新</ref>。
{{See also|函館の陸繋砂州}}
砂山には[[ハマナス]]が咲き美しい景観が広がっていた。戦前、[[青函連絡船]]通信用の無線送信所があったが、[[函館大火|昭和9年函館大火]]で焼失。また、貧しい人々が砂山に穴を掘って建てた掘立小屋の並ぶ「サムライ部落」「砂山部落」と呼ばれる地域であったが、戦後、[[砂鉄]]を取るため砂山は取り崩され、掘立小屋も立ち退きにより、現在はその面影はない<ref name=column48/>。[[明治時代]]、[[亀田村]]・[[鍛冶村]]経由のルートと並び函館区と[[湯川町 (北海道)#大字下湯川村|下湯川村]]（のちの[[湯川町 (北海道)|湯川町]]）を結ぶルートの一つであったが、[[1887年]]（明治20年）に[[亀田村]]から直接向かうルートが開通した。 函館市内を流れる[[亀田川]]は元々[[函館港]]側に[[河口]]があったが、年々土砂を運び港を浅くし、[[1877年]]（明治10年）ごろから[[コレラ]]など[[伝染病]]が出始めたので、[[1883年]]（明治16年）に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]に雇われていた[[オランダ]]の[[土木工学|土木技師]][[ローウェンホルスト・ムルデル]]（Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder）の提案と設計により、1888年（明治21年）に大森浜へ切り替えられた。
1934年（昭和9年）[[3月21日]]、[[函館大火]]が起き、この浜へ避難した人々が炎と激浪の挟み撃ちになって逃げ場を失い[[溺死]]、または[[凍死]]した者が多数出た。
=== 年表 === * [[1882年]]（[[明治]]15年）8月 - [[青森県]][[東津軽郡]][[一本木村]]（現・[[今別町]]）との間に[[海底ケーブル|海底通信ケーブル]]が敷設された * [[1885年]]（明治18年） - [[海水浴場]]開設 * [[1888年]]（明治21年）- 亀田川切り替え。河口になる * [[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）3月21日 - 昭和9年函館大火被災。多数の死者が出る。また青函連絡船無線送信所が焼失 * [[1954年]]（昭和29年） - 遊泳禁止区域に指定され海水浴場が廃止される * [[1958年]]（昭和33年） - 啄木小公園開園
== 動植物 == 植物 * [[ハマナス]]<ref>北海道 NEWS WEB [https://www3.nhk.or.jp/sapporo-news/20240606/7000067513.html "啄木が愛した海岸 ハマナスの花が見頃 函館"] NHK 2024年6月6日18:35更新 2025年3月5日閲覧</ref> * [[ハマヒルガオ]]<ref name="スナガニ2017"/>
;動物 * [[スナガニ]]<ref name="スナガニ2017"/>
== 公共施設等 == [[1882年]]（明治15年）8月に[[青森県]][[東津軽郡]][[一本木村]]（現・[[今別町]]）との間に[[海底ケーブル|海底通信ケーブル]]が敷設された<ref>"日本での海底通信ケーブル年表" NTTワールドエンジニアリングマリン 2024年3月2日閲覧</ref>。[[1885年]]（明治18年）には[[海水浴場]]が設けられたが、[[1954年]]（昭和29年）に事故防止で遊泳禁止区域となったために廃止された<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm 市史余話75 啄木も見た大森浜海水浴場] - 函館市中央図書館</ref><ref name="フィルムコミッション"/>。
== 文化 == [[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）に[[石川啄木]]が函館に移り住んだ際に、大森浜と砂山を愛し歌を詠んだことにちなみ、[[1958年]]（昭和33年）、大森浜沿いに「啄木小公園」が整備され、[[本郷新]]作の銅像が置かれている<ref name=column48/>。
== 交通 == ; 路線バス * [[函館バス]]95系統（[[函館市企業局交通部|函館市交通局]]6系統→函館バス6系統）「函館駅前 - 日吉営業所前」 * 函館バス95A系統「湯川中学校前 - 函館駅前」 * 函館バス96系統「函館駅前 - [[函館空港]]」行き
; [[札幌市|札幌]]方面より * [[北海道中央バス]]・[[道南バス]]・[[北都交通 (北海道)|北都交通]]・[[函館バス]]「[[高速はこだて号]]」
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[函館の陸繋砂州]]
== 参考文献 == * 自治体史 ** 函館市総務部函館市史編さん室編 『函館市史』 函館市 *** 通説編第4巻 2002年 *** 銭亀沢編 1998年 * 商業誌 ** {{Cite book|和書 |editor=北海道新聞社|editor-link=北海道新聞社 |coauthors = |translator = |others = |title = はこだて歴史散歩 |edition = |origyear = |year = |publisher = |series = |isbn = 9784893633156 |pages = }}
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html 石川啄木座像（啄木小公園）] - 函館市公式観光情報"はこぶら"
{{Japan-geo-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおもりはま}} [[Category:日本の海岸]] [[Category:北海道の地形]] [[Category:函館市の地理]] [[Category:津軽海峡]]

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|函館山・立待岬から見た大森浜（[[2003年]]9月）]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=大森浜・啄木小公園付近|width=250}}
'''大森浜'''（おおもりはま）は、[[北海道]][[函館市]]の[[海岸]]である。
== 概要 == [[函館山]]を[[陸繋島]]とし、[[山麓]]より千代台の[[台地]]の縁（[[函館段丘]]）までの長さ約3,000m、幅約600m（[[埋立地]]を除く）の[[陸繋砂州]]<ref name="函館市史銭亀沢編_152-153">函館市史 銭亀沢編 pp.152-153</ref>の東側<ref>函館市史通説編第4巻 pp.839-843</ref>、[[住吉漁港]]より[[湯の川温泉 (北海道)|湯の川温泉]]、[[松倉川]][[河口]]付近までの[[津軽海峡]]に面していている[[海浜]]である<ref name="フィルムコミッション">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "大森浜"] はこだてフィルムコミッション 2024年3月2日閲覧</ref>。長さ約6,000m、最大幅約70mで干満差は約1.1メートル<ref name="スナガニ2017">五嶋聖治 "北限のスナガニの季節的な砂浜利用パターン：啄木はスナガニに出会えたか？" 日本ベントス学会誌 71: 83–89 2017年 pp.83-89</ref>。
かつて、この一帯（現在の高盛町、日の出町）には津軽海峡から運ばれてできた高さ30メートル、東西1キロメートル、南北300メートルもの<ref name=column48>[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm 『函館市史』通説編第4巻 コラム48「大森浜の砂山」] - 公立はこだて未来大学</ref>[[砂丘]]「[[大森山砂丘]]」があって<ref name="函館市史銭亀沢編_152-153"/>、大森は、その砂の盛り上がる「'''大盛り'''」より名付けられた<ref>函館市観光部編 [https://www.hakobura.jp/spots/607 "大森浜（啄木小公園）"] はこぶら 2025年3月7日閲覧</ref>。[[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）の今でも砂が風で巻き上げられ周辺の道路や駐車場に積もる現象がみられる<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "雨・風強まる函館市内からの報告 砂浜から巻き上げられた砂がたまって駐車場が…"] 北海道テレビ放送 2023年11月17日更新</ref>。
{{See also|函館の陸繋砂州}}
砂山には[[ハマナス]]が咲き美しい景観が広がっていた。戦前、[[青函連絡船]]通信用の無線送信所があったが、[[函館大火|昭和9年函館大火]]で焼失。また、貧しい人々が砂山に穴を掘って建てた掘立小屋の並ぶ「サムライ部落」「砂山部落」と呼ばれる地域であったが、戦後、[[砂鉄]]を取るため砂山は取り崩され、掘立小屋も立ち退きにより、現在はその面影はない<ref name=column48/>。[[明治時代]]、[[亀田村]]・[[鍛冶村]]経由のルートと並び函館区と[[湯川町 (北海道)#大字下湯川村|下湯川村]]（のちの[[湯川町 (北海道)|湯川町]]）を結ぶルートの一つであったが、[[1887年]]（明治20年）に[[亀田村]]から直接向かうルートが開通した。 函館市内を流れる[[亀田川]]は元々[[函館港]]側に[[河口]]があったが、年々土砂を運び港を浅くし、[[1877年]]（明治10年）ごろから[[コレラ]]など[[伝染病]]が出始めたので、[[1883年]]（明治16年）に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]に雇われていた[[オランダ]]の[[土木工学|土木技師]][[ローウェンホルスト・ムルデル]]（Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder）の提案と設計により、1888年（明治21年）に大森浜へ切り替えられた。
1934年（昭和9年）[[3月21日]]、[[函館大火]]が起き、この浜へ避難した人々が炎と激浪の挟み撃ちになって逃げ場を失い[[溺死]]、または[[凍死]]した者が多数出た。
=== 年表 === * [[1882年]]（[[明治]]15年）8月 - [[青森県]][[東津軽郡]][[一本木村]]（現・[[今別町]]）との間に[[海底ケーブル|海底通信ケーブル]]が敷設された * [[1885年]]（明治18年） - [[海水浴場]]開設 * [[1888年]]（明治21年）- 亀田川切り替え。河口になる * [[1934年]]（[[昭和]]9年）3月21日 - 昭和9年函館大火被災。多数の死者が出る。また青函連絡船無線送信所が焼失 * [[1954年]]（昭和29年） - 遊泳禁止区域に指定され海水浴場が廃止される * [[1958年]]（昭和33年） - 啄木小公園開園
== 動植物 == 植物 * [[ハマナス]]<ref>北海道 NEWS WEB [https://www3.nhk.or.jp/sapporo-news/20240606/7000067513.html "啄木が愛した海岸 ハマナスの花が見頃 函館"] NHK 2024年6月6日18:35更新 2025年3月5日閲覧</ref> * [[ハマヒルガオ]]<ref name="スナガニ2017"/>
;動物 * [[スナガニ]]<ref name="スナガニ2017"/>
== 公共施設等 == [[1882年]]（明治15年）8月に[[青森県]][[東津軽郡]][[一本木村]]（現・[[今別町]]）との間に[[海底ケーブル|海底通信ケーブル]]が敷設された<ref>"日本での海底通信ケーブル年表" NTTワールドエンジニアリングマリン 2024年3月2日閲覧</ref>。[[1885年]]（明治18年）には[[海水浴場]]が設けられたが、[[1954年]]（昭和29年）に事故防止で遊泳禁止区域となったために廃止された<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm 市史余話75 啄木も見た大森浜海水浴場] - 函館市中央図書館</ref><ref name="フィルムコミッション"/>。
== 文化 == [[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）に[[石川啄木]]が函館に移り住んだ際に、大森浜と砂山を愛し歌を詠んだことにちなみ、[[1958年]]（昭和33年）、大森浜沿いに「啄木小公園」が整備され、[[本郷新]]作の銅像が置かれている<ref name=column48/>。
== 交通 == ; 路線バス * [[函館バス]]95系統（[[函館市企業局交通部|函館市交通局]]6系統→函館バス6系統）「函館駅前 - 日吉営業所前」 * 函館バス95A系統「湯川中学校前 - 函館駅前」 * 函館バス96系統「函館駅前 - [[函館空港]]」行き
; [[札幌市|札幌]]方面より * [[北海道中央バス]]・[[道南バス]]・[[北都交通 (北海道)|北都交通]]・[[函館バス]]「[[高速はこだて号]]」
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[函館の陸繋砂州]]
== 参考文献 == * 自治体史 ** 函館市総務部函館市史編さん室編 『函館市史』 函館市 *** 通説編第4巻 2002年 *** 銭亀沢編 1998年 * 商業誌 ** {{Cite book|和書 |editor=北海道新聞社|editor-link=北海道新聞社 |coauthors = |translator = |others = |title = はこだて歴史散歩 |edition = |origyear = |year = |publisher = |series = |isbn = 9784893633156 |pages = }}
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html 石川啄木座像（啄木小公園）] - 函館市公式観光情報"はこぶら"
{{Japan-geo-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおもりはま}} [[Category:日本の海岸]] [[Category:北海道の地形]] [[Category:函館市の地理]] [[Category:津軽海峡]]

revision.text:
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
{{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|大森浜|version=104006415|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}

== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. '''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Culture == In [[1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

revision.text:
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[coastline]] in [[Hakodate]], [[Hokkaido]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[Meiji period]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[Hokkaido]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

<!-- {{Commonscat|}} --><!--Ōmori Beach--><!--Ōmori Beach--><!--Ōmori Beach--> [[Category:Landforms of Hokkaido]] [[Category:Coasts of Japan]] [[Category:Tsugaru Strait]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

<!-- {{Commonscat|}} --><!--Ōmori Beach--><!--Ōmori Beach--><!--Ōmori Beach--> [[Category:Landforms of Hokkaido]] [[Category:Coasts of Japan]] [[Category:Tsugaru Strait]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
{{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|大森浜|version=104006415|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 6]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11436381]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
{{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11436381]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
{{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
{{Japan-geo-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11436381]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
