revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11436381]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ([[:en:2003|2003]] September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"

The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.

== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>

In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in [[:en:Hakodate|Hakodate]], [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the [[:en:Tsugaru Strait|Tsugaru Strait]] from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the [[:en:Shimokita Peninsula|Shimokita Peninsula]] across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in [[:en:Rosa rugosa|Rosa rugosa]], providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the [[:en:Seikan Ferry|Seikan Ferry]] operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in [[:en:1887|1887]]. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in [[:en:1888|1888]] due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of [[:en:Cholera|Cholera]] and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * [[:en:1882|1882]] (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * [[:en:1885|1885]] (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * [[:en:1888|1888]] (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * [[:en:1954|1954]] (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In [[:en:1882|1882]], an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in [[:en:1885|1885]], it was closed in [[:en:1954|1954]] due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In [[:en:1907|1907]] (Meiji 40), [[:en:Takuboku Ishikawa|Takuboku Ishikawa]] moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In [[:en:1958|1958]], Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - [[:en:Hakodate Airport|Hakodate Airport]]"
; From [[:en:Sapporo|Sapporo]] * [[:en:Hokkaido Chuo Bus|Hokkaido Chuo Bus]], [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==

== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"

=== Timeline === * {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882}} (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}} (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * {{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888}} (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954}} (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}} (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882}}, an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}}, it was closed in {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954}} due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907}} (Meiji 40), {{ill|Takuboku Ishikawa|en|Takuboku Ishikawa|WD=Q467747|lt=Takuboku Ishikawa}} moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}}, Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]]
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ({{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003}} September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in {{ill|Hakodate|en|Hakodate|WD=Q26418|lt=Hakodate}}, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the {{ill|Tsugaru Strait|en|Tsugaru Strait|WD=Q271521|lt=Tsugaru Strait}} from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of {{ill|Yunokawa Onsen|en|Yunokawa Onsen|WD=Q8061358|lt=Yunokawa Onsen}} and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the {{ill|Shimokita Peninsula|en|Shimokita Peninsula|WD=Q1321564|lt=Shimokita Peninsula}} across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in {{ill|Rosa rugosa|en|Rosa rugosa|WD=Q634975|lt=Rosa rugosa}}, providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the {{ill|Seikan Ferry|en|Seikan Ferry|WD=Q11661109|lt=Seikan Ferry}} operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887}}. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in {{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888}} due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of {{ill|Cholera|en|Cholera|WD=Q12090|lt=Cholera}} and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882}} (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}} (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * {{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888}} (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954}} (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}} (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882}}, an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885}}, it was closed in {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954}} due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907}} (Meiji 40), {{ill|Takuboku Ishikawa|en|Takuboku Ishikawa|WD=Q467747|lt=Takuboku Ishikawa}} moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}}, Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - {{ill|Hakodate Airport|en|Hakodate Airport|WD=Q1155685|lt=Hakodate Airport}}"
; From {{ill|Sapporo|en|Sapporo|WD=Q37951|lt=Sapporo}} * {{ill|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|en|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|WD=Q1284698|lt=Hokkaido Chuo Bus}}, [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"

; From {{ill|Sapporo|en|Sapporo|WD=Q37951|lt=Sapporo|1=Sapporo}} * {{ill|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|en|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|WD=Q1284698|lt=Hokkaido Chuo Bus|1=Hokkaido Chuo Bus}}, [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大森浜]]
[[File:Tachimachimisaki in Hakodate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ōmori Beach as seen from Cape Tachimachi on Mount Hakodate ({{ill|2003|en|2003|WD=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} September)]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|41.7716|140.750828}}|caption=Around Ōmori Beach and Takuboku Park|width=250}}
'''Ōmori Beach''' (大森浜) is a [[:en:coastline|coastline]] in {{ill|Hakodate|en|Hakodate|WD=Q26418|lt=Hakodate|1=Hakodate}}, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan.
== Overview == Ōmori Beach stretches along the {{ill|Tsugaru Strait|en|Tsugaru Strait|WD=Q271521|lt=Tsugaru Strait|1=Tsugaru Strait}} from [[Sumiyoshi Fishing Port]] to the vicinity of {{ill|Yunokawa Onsen|en|Yunokawa Onsen|WD=Q8061358|lt=Yunokawa Onsen|1=Yunokawa Onsen}} and the mouth of the [[Matsukura River]]. On clear days, it offers scenic views of the {{ill|Shimokita Peninsula|en|Shimokita Peninsula|WD=Q1321564|lt=Shimokita Peninsula|1=Shimokita Peninsula}} across the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="Film Commission">[http://www.hakodate-fc.com/database2.php?mode=detail&id=188 "Ōmori Beach"] Hakodate Film Commission, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref>
In the past, this area (now Takamori-chō and Hinode-chō) featured a massive sand dune 30 meters high, 1 kilometer wide, and 300 meters deep, formed by sand carried from the Tsugaru Strait.<ref name="column48">[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_04/shishi_07-02/shishi_07-02-48.htm "The Sand Dunes of Ōmori Beach," Hakodate City History, Volume 4] - Hakodate Future University.</ref> Ōmori ("large mound") derives its name from the prominent sand formation. Even today, windblown sand accumulates on nearby roads and parking lots.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iaBwtQI0xik "Strong winds in Hakodate scatter sand from the beach"] Hokkaido Television Broadcasting, updated November 17, 2023.</ref>
The sand dunes were once covered in {{ill|Rosa rugosa|en|Rosa rugosa|WD=Q634975|lt=Rosa rugosa|1=Rosa rugosa}}, providing a beautiful landscape. Before the [[Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire]] in 1934, a radio transmission station for the {{ill|Seikan Ferry|en|Seikan Ferry|WD=Q11661109|lt=Seikan Ferry|1=Seikan Ferry}} operated here but was destroyed in the disaster. The area was also known as "Samurai Village" or "Sand Hill Village," where impoverished people built huts in the dunes. After the war, the sand dunes were mined for [[:en:Iron sand|Iron sand]], and the huts were removed, erasing the area's former appearance.<ref name="column48"/>
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the area served as a route connecting Hakodate and [[Shimoyukawa Village]] (later [[Yukawa Town]]), along with a route via [[Kameda Village]] and [[Kaji Village]]. However, a direct route from Kameda Village opened in {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887|1=1887}}. The [[Kameda River]], which originally flowed into [[Hakodate Port]], was rerouted to Ōmori Beach in {{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888|1=1888}} due to concerns about sediment accumulation and the spread of {{ill|Cholera|en|Cholera|WD=Q12090|lt=Cholera|1=Cholera}} and other infectious diseases. This project was proposed and designed by Dutch civil engineer [[Anthonie Thomas Lubertus Rouwenhorst Mulder]] under Japan's [[:en:Ministry of the Interior|Ministry of the Interior]].<ref name="column48"/>
On [[March 21, 1934]], the Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire forced many evacuees to the beach, where they were trapped between flames and fierce waves. Many perished from drowning or freezing.
=== Timeline === * {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882|1=1882}} (Meiji 15) August - An [[:en:undersea cable|undersea cable]] was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]. * {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885|1=1885}} (Meiji 18) - A [[:en:beach resort|beach resort]] was established. * {{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888|1=1888}} (Meiji 21) - The Kameda River's course was altered, creating a new estuary at Ōmori Beach. * {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}} (Shōwa 9) March 21 - The Shōwa 9 Hakodate Fire devastated the area. * {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954|1=1954}} (Shōwa 29) - The beach resort was closed, and swimming was prohibited for safety reasons. * {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}} (Shōwa 33) - Takuboku Park opened.
== Public Facilities == In {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882|1=1882}}, an undersea cable was laid between [[Honshu]] and [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].<ref>"Chronology of Undersea Communication Cables in Japan," NTT World Engineering Marine, accessed March 2, 2024.</ref> Although a beach resort was opened in {{ill|1885|en|1885|WD=Q7821|lt=1885|1=1885}}, it was closed in {{ill|1954|en|1954|WD=Q18581|lt=1954|1=1954}} due to safety concerns.<ref>[http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/yowa/yowa_contents/yowa_075.htm "Takuboku and Ōmori Beach Swimming Resort"] - Hakodate Central Library.</ref><ref name="Film Commission"/>
== Culture == In {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (Meiji 40), {{ill|Takuboku Ishikawa|en|Takuboku Ishikawa|WD=Q467747|lt=Takuboku Ishikawa|1=Takuboku Ishikawa}} moved to Hakodate and was inspired by the beauty of Ōmori Beach and its sand dunes, leading him to compose poetry. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}}, Takuboku Park was established along the beach, featuring a bronze statue of the poet by [[Shin Hongo]].<ref name="column48"/>
== Transportation == ; Bus Routes * [[Hakodate Bus]] Route 95: "Hakodate Station - Hiyoshi Depot" * Hakodate Bus Route 95A: "Yunokawa Junior High School - Hakodate Station" * Hakodate Bus Route 96: "Hakodate Station - {{ill|Hakodate Airport|en|Hakodate Airport|WD=Q1155685|lt=Hakodate Airport|1=Hakodate Airport}}"
; From {{ill|Sapporo|en|Sapporo|WD=Q37951|lt=Sapporo|1=Sapporo}} * {{ill|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|en|Hokkaido Chuo Bus|WD=Q1284698|lt=Hokkaido Chuo Bus|1=Hokkaido Chuo Bus}}, [[Dōnan Bus]], [[Hokuto Kotsu]], and [[Hakodate Bus]] "[[Highway Hakodate-go]]"
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
{{Section-stub}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|language=ja |editor=Hokkaido Shimbun |title=Hakodate Historical Walk |isbn=9784893633156}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/11/post-199.html Takuboku Statue (Takuboku Park)] - Hakodate Official Tourism Information "Hakobura"
